messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] Responsible AI in Academic Research — a policy proposal
messages.subject_clean: Responsible AI in Academic Research — a policy proposal
messages.body_plain:
Hello colleagues

I'm sharing a new open-access policy document that you may find
useful: Responsible
AI in Academic Research: A Competency Framework for Research Training
<https://instats.org/publications/responsible-ai-in-academic-research>.

It came from a deceptively simple question: what does "responsible AI use"
actually mean at the level of everyday research practice? This seemed like
a priority given the substantial AI research training
<https://instats.org/results?nav=On-Demand&view=Seminars> that Instats has
been offering since ChatGPT's launch. Over time, the document grew into a
survey of AI policy at 38 top-tier doctoral universities across 15
countries, only six of which currently go beyond research-integrity rules
into AI literacy and valid research practice. The piece sets out a
five-dimension competency framework and a plain-language maturity grid that
any department, graduate school, or research office can use to see where it
stands today and what a credible next step looks like. A key takeaway is that plagiarism is not the central issue AI raises for research training. The deeper concern is whether research remains valid, and whether our training equips students with the knowledge and skills to conduct it responsibly.

The policy document is written for the people actually making these calls ?
supervisors, deans, graduate-school directors, and research-integrity
committees ? and it's free to read, share, and download. Please feel free
to pass it along to anyone wrestling with these questions.


Best wishes

Michael Zyphur
Professor and Director
Instats | instats.org

	[[alternative HTML version deleted]]

I'm sharing a new open-access policy document that you may find

AI in Academic Research: A Competency Framework for Research Training
<https://instats.org/publica

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] markinp: a small CLI to validate & build Program MARK .inp files (complements RMark)
messages.subject_clean: markinp: a small CLI to validate & build Program MARK .inp files (complements RMark)
messages.body_plain:
Dear list,
I'd like to share a small open-source tool that may be useful to anyone who
works with Program
MARK input (.inp) files, including RMark users: markinp.

Up front: it is a command-line tool / Python library, not an R package, and
it is an independent, unofficial utility (not affiliated with MARK or
RMark). It deliberately does no statistics ? file I/O and validation only.

Why it might help an RMark workflow:
- It gives precise, line-numbered diagnostics when an .inp is malformed,
instead of a cryptic rejection ? useful as a pre-flight check before
convert.inp() / importing data.
- It builds a valid .inp from a tidy CSV (long or wide), handling groups,
individual covariates, frequencies, and comments deterministically ? so you
don't hand-edit in a text editor or Excel.
- Being a CLI with proper exit codes, it drops into a Makefile, CI job, or
pre-commit hook to guard a data pipeline (a reusable GitHub Action and
pre-commit hook are included). No R or Python knowledge needed to run
markinp validate data/*.inp.

Example:
$ markinp validate captures.inp
MK004  ERROR  line 7: record has 3 frequency values but the file uses 2
groups
       hint: Give exactly one frequency per group; check for a
missing/extra value

Scope: the standard 0/1 encounter-history format is fully validated;
known-fate, dead-recovery, multistrata and occupancy formats are currently
detected and structurally checked only (the tool tells you when it can only
partially validate a file). A round-trip .inp ? tidy-dataframe converter
(mirroring convert.inp, both directions) is on the roadmap.

Install: pip install markinp
Source / docs / issues (MIT): https://github.com/leonbzt/markinp

Feedback, feature requests, and awkward real-world .inp files are all very
welcome.

Best regards,
Leon Botzenhardt

	[[alternative HTML version deleted]]

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] Take your R Skills to the next level this Summer
messages.subject_clean: Take your R Skills to the next level this Summer
messages.body_plain:
Dear all,
 
Whether you're cleaning complex datasets, building reproducible analyses, developing your own R packages, or exploring the latest AI tools, mastering R can dramatically improve the quality and efficiency of your research.
 
This July, Physalia is offering a series of live, hands-on online courses designed to help researchers and data scientists strengthen their R programming and statistical analysis skills. 
Upcoming Courses
- [ Advanced Multivariate Modelling in R (GLLVMs) ]( https://www.physalia-courses.org/courses-workshops/gllvm/ )
online, 6?10 July
Learn modern statistical approaches for analysing complex multivariate ecological and biological data.
 
-  [ Dealing with Messy Data in R ]( https://www.physalia-courses.org/courses-workshops/messy-data/ )
online, 13?15 July
Transform untidy, inconsistent datasets into clean, analysis-ready data using efficient R workflows.
 
 - [ Time-series Analysis and Forecasting in R ]( https://www.physalia-courses.org/courses-workshops/time-series-in-r/ )
online, 20?24 July
Model, visualise, and forecast temporal data using powerful statistical methods and R packages.
 
- [ Reproducibility with R ]( https://www.physalia-courses.org/courses-workshops/r-reproducibility/ )
online, 20?23 July
Build transparent, reproducible workflows using Quarto, Git, project management, and workflow automation.
 
- [ Developing R/Bioconductor Packages ]( https://www.physalia-courses.org/courses-workshops/r-packages/ )
online, 27?31 July
Learn how to create, document, test, and distribute professional R and Bioconductor packages.
 
- [ Integrating LLMs into R Workflows ]( https://www.physalia-courses.org/courses-workshops/llms-4-r-fsbi/ )
online, 4 August
Discover how AI and Large Language Models can assist with coding, documentation, data exploration, and reproducible research?all from within R.
 
 
 
We look forward to learning with you this summer!
Best wishes,
Carlo
 
 
 

--------------------

Carlo Pecoraro, Ph.D


Physalia-courses DIRECTOR

info at physalia-courses.org

mobile: +49 17645230846

[ Bluesky ]( https://bsky.app/profile/physaliacourses.bsky.social ) [ Linkedin ]( https://www.linkedin.com/in/physalia-courses-a64418127/ )

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] Live Online Course: Introduction to GIS with QGIS
messages.subject_clean: Live Online Course: Introduction to GIS with QGIS
messages.body_plain:
Dear colleagues,

A few spots are available on the live online course "QGIS: An 
Introduction to Geographic Information Systems (GIS)".

Course webpage: 
https://www.transmittingscience.com/courses/ecology/qgis-an-introduction-to-geographic-information-systems/

This course is designed to introduce participants to the world of 
Geographic Information Systems (GIS) using QGIS, a free and open-source 
software widely used for spatial analysis.

By the end of the course, participants will be able to:

  	* Demonstrate an understanding of the core principles of Geographic 
Information Systems
  	* Navigate the QGIS interface and effectively operate its main tools 
and functionalities
  	* Import, visualise, and manage different types of geospatial data
  	* Perform basic spatial analyses
  	* Create thematic maps and customise data symbology

	* Export maps and results for presentations or reports

For any questions, please write to courses at transmittingscience.com

Best regards,

Haris
-- 
Haris Saslis, PhD
Course Coordinator
Transmitting Science
www.transmittingscience.com [1]

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] StatSuite: Interactive R applications for statistics and ecology
messages.subject_clean: StatSuite: Interactive R applications for statistics and ecology
messages.body_plain:
Dear R-sig-ecology members,

I would like to share StatSuite, a collection of interactive web
applications developed entirely in R to support statistical analysis, study
design, and ecological data analysis without requiring programming
experience.

Although the applications are designed for users with little or no coding
background, every analysis includes the corresponding reproducible R code,
allowing users to learn from the workflow and reproduce the analyses in
their own R environment.

The project is primarily available in Spanish, so it may be particularly
useful for Spanish-speaking students, researchers, practitioners, and
instructors looking for accessible teaching and analysis tools based on R.

StatSuite currently includes applications covering topics such as:

* Study design and sampling.
* Exploratory data analysis and statistical graphics.
* Frequentist and Bayesian statistical modeling.
* Machine learning.
* Spatial data analysis.
* Ecological applications, including occupancy models, abundance models,
biological monitoring, species distribution modeling using H3 hexagonal
grids, and multivariate community analysis.
* Text mining for open-ended survey responses.

A description of each application, together with access links, is available
at:

https://statsuite.netlify.app

I hope these tools may be useful for teaching, training, and applied
ecological research, particularly within the Spanish-speaking R community.

Comments and suggestions are always welcome.

Best regards,

Manuel Sp?nola

-- 
*Manuel Sp?nola, Ph.D.*
Instituto Internacional en Conservaci?n y Manejo de Vida Silvestre
Universidad Nacional
Apartado 1350-3000
Heredia
COSTA RICA
mspinola at una.cr <mspinola at una.ac.cr>
mspinola10 at gmail.com
Tel?fono: (506) 8706 - 4662
Sitio web institucional: ICOMVIS
<http://www.icomvis.una.ac.cr/index.php/manuel>
Sitio web personal: Sitio personal <https://mspinola-sitioweb.netlify.app>
Blog sobre Ciencia de Datos: Blog de Ciencia de Datos
<https://mspinola-ciencia-de-datos.netlify.app>

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] Time Series Analysis and Forecasting in R course - online, 20-24 July
messages.subject_clean: Time Series Analysis and Forecasting in R course - online, 20-24 July
messages.body_plain:
Good morning everyone,
There is still time to join our online course Time Series Analysis and Forecasting in R, taking place 20?24 July: [ https://www.physalia-courses.org/courses-workshops/time-series-in-r/ ]( https://www.physalia-courses.org/courses-workshops/time-series-in-r/ ) 
 
Over five interactive online sessions (16:00?19:00, Berlin time), participants will learn how to analyse, model, and forecast ecological time series using modern Bayesian approaches in R.
 
During the course, participants will learn how to:
wrangle, visualize, and explore time series data in R,
build dynamic GLMs and GAMs using the {mvgam} and {brms} packages,
model nonlinear effects, temporal autocorrelation, and dynamic processes,
evaluate and compare forecasting models, and
generate accurate forecasts for univariate and multivariate ecological time series.
The course combines lectures with live coding and hands-on practical exercises. All course materials, including presentations, datasets, and fully commented R scripts, will be provided electronically.
 
 
Should you have any questions, please feel free to contact us.
 
Best regards,
Carlo
--------------------

Carlo Pecoraro, Ph.D


Physalia-courses DIRECTOR

info at physalia-courses.org

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] Online course: Electoral Geography and Spatial Causal Inference
messages.subject_clean: Online course: Electoral Geography and Spatial Causal Inference
messages.body_plain:
Hello R-SIG-ECOLOGY members

We are pleased to announce an upcoming interactive seminar on advanced spatial analysis techniques. Electoral Geography & Spatial Causal Inference is a 2-day seminar livestreaming July 29?30, led by Fernando De la Cuesta (Department of Applied, Public and Political Economics, Complutense University of Madrid). This seminar introduces researchers to advanced spatial causal inference techniques, showing how to model geographic distance and exploit spatial boundaries as quasi-experiments in R. Environmental and ecological researchers can apply these methods to evaluate the impact of localized policy interventions, habitat boundaries, or spatial exposure on ecological outcomes. Participants will gain hands-on experience with distance-based modeling and regression discontinuity designs, leaving with reusable R scripts to analyze their own spatial datasets.

Sign up today (https://instats.org/seminar/electoral-geography-and-spatial-causal-i) to secure your spot, and please share this opportunity with colleagues and students who might benefit!


Best wishes

Michael Zyphur
Professor and Director
Instats | instats.org
	[[alternative HTML version deleted]]

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] Online Course: Building Reproducible Data Analysis Workflows in R
messages.subject_clean: Online Course: Building Reproducible Data Analysis Workflows in R
messages.body_plain:
Dear all,
 
Reproducibility is a cornerstone of good science, yet many analyses remain difficult to reproduce due to missing data, undocumented workflows, software dependencies, and poorly organised code. This practical course will provide participants with the tools and skills needed to overcome these challenges in R.
 
Dates: Online, 20-23 July 
 
Course website: [ https://www.physalia-courses.org/courses-workshops/r-reproducibility/ ]( https://www.physalia-courses.org/courses-workshops/r-reproducibility/ ) 
 
 
Through lectures, live coding, and hands-on exercises, participants will learn how to organise projects, document analyses, manage software dependencies, and share code and results effectively. The course will introduce key tools from the R ecosystem, including Quarto for reproducible documents, Git and GitHub for version control and collaboration, renv for managing package dependencies, and Docker for creating reproducible computational environments.
 
Participants will also learn how to structure analyses as research compendia?a recommended approach for organising code, data, and documentation in a reproducible format.
 
By the end of the course, participants will have a practical workflow for developing reproducible research projects that are easier to share, maintain, and build upon, strengthening collaboration and improving the reliability of scientific results.
 
 
Best regards,
 
Carlo
 
 
 
--------------------

Carlo Pecoraro, Ph.D


Physalia-courses DIRECTOR

info at physalia-courses.org

mobile: +49 17645230846

[ Bluesky ]( https://bsky.app/profile/physaliacourses.bsky.social ) [ Linkedin ]( https://www.linkedin.com/in/physalia-courses-a64418127/ )

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] (Online live training): Design, Implement and Promote Species Monitoring in R
messages.subject_clean: (Online live training): Design, Implement and Promote Species Monitoring in R
messages.body_plain:
Dear all,
 
Join our online course "Design, Implement and Promote Species Monitoring in R" from 19?21 October.
 
In just three interactive mornings (09:00?12:30, Berlin time), you'll learn how to design robust monitoring protocols, select the right analytical approach, and analyse monitoring data in R using real-world examples and fully annotated scripts.
 
The course covers:
Capture-Mark-Recapture (CMR)
Distance Sampling
Site Occupancy
N-mixture Models
Ideal for students, researchers, conservation practitioners, and NGO professionals. Basic knowledge of R is helpful.
 
Learn more and register:[ https://www.physalia-courses.org/courses-workshops/species-monitoring-in-r/ ]( https://www.physalia-courses.org/courses-workshops/species-monitoring-in-r/ )
 
Best regards,

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] Live online course: Evolutionary Biogeography
messages.body_plain:
Dear colleagues,

Transmitting Science is offering a new edition of the live online course 
"Model-Based Statistical Inference in Evolutionary Biogeography".

Course webpage:

https://www.transmittingscience.com/courses/evolution/model-based-statistical-inference-evolutionary-biogeography-2/ 
[1]

Course overview:

This course offers a structured, hands-on introduction to methods for 
inferring historical biogeography using phylogenetic and spatial data.

Participants will learn how to estimate ancestral geographic ranges, 
compare probabilistic models, and interpret biogeographic patterns using 
real data.

The course moves from introductory training in R, phylogenies, and 
BioGeoBEARS, through likelihood-based model testing and visualisation, 
to advanced topics such as trait-dependent dispersal, state-dependent 
diversification models, and large-scale analyses. It also incorporates 
the integration of GIS and paleogeographic data (e.g., via GPlates), 
equipping participants with a comprehensive toolkit for studying 
lineages across space and time.

For any questions, please write to courses at transmittingscience.com

Best regards,

Haris

-- 
Haris Saslis, PhD
Course Coordinator
Transmitting Science
www.transmittingscience.com [2]



Links:
------
[1] 
https://www.transmittingscience.com/courses/evolution/model-based-statistical-inference-evolutionary-biogeography-2/
[2] http://www.transmittingscience.com

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] (Online course) - Species Distribution and Ecological Niche Modelling in R
messages.subject_clean: (Online course) - Species Distribution and Ecological Niche Modelling in R
messages.body_plain:
Dear all,
 
Our online course ?Species Distribution and Ecological Niche Modelling in R?, taking place 14?18 September, is almost full.
 
If you are interested in strengthening your ecological modelling skills and learn how to harness the power of R for understanding species distributions, check it out: [ https://www.physalia-courses.org/courses-workshops/course45/ ]( https://www.physalia-courses.org/courses-workshops/course45/ )
 
 
Through a combination of live online sessions and guided practical exercises, participants will work with species occurrence data of their choice and develop the skills needed to apply SDM and ENM for describing, predicting, and projecting species distributions across space and time.
 
By the end of the course, participants will be able to:
Understand the fundamental concepts behind SDM and ENM
Design, build, and evaluate models using automated R workflows
Recognise the strengths and limitations of different modelling approaches
Apply models to a range of ecological questions and scenarios
 
Best regards,
Carlo

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] Causal Roadmaps for Environmental Data
messages.body_plain:
Hello R-SIG-ECOLOGY members

Strengthen the design and interpretation of causal questions in observational research. Data Can Deceive: Causal Roadmaps is a seminar livestreaming August 5, led by Kathryn Morrison (Precision Analytics) and Andy Wilson (Spencer Fox Eccles School of Medicine, University of Utah). Formulate defensible causal estimands, target-trial protocols, and directed acyclic graphs for observational ecological and environmental studies. Assess confounding, inappropriate adjustment, and identification assumptions when analysing vegetation, soils, hydrology, or other real-world data with R. Apply roadmap-based design and targeted learning to document transparent, reproducible analytic choices for collaborators and reviewers.

Sign up today (https://instats.org/seminar/data-can-deceive-causal-roadmaps) to secure your spot, and please share this opportunity with colleagues and students who might benefit!


Best wishes

Michael Zyphur
Professor and Director
Instats | instats.org
	[[alternative HTML version deleted]]

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] Live online course: Evaluating SDMs in R
messages.subject_clean: Live online course: Evaluating SDMs in R
messages.body_plain:
Dear colleagues,

Transmitting Science is offering the course "Evaluating and Validating 
Species Distribution Models with R".

Course webpage:
https://www.transmittingscience.com/courses/ecology/evaluating-and-validating-species-distribution-models-with-r/ 
[1]

The aim of this course is to introduce you to evaluation and validation 
methods for species distribution models (SDMs) using R. The course will 
cover a wide range of model-evaluation metrics (discrimination, 
classification, explanatory power, calibration), and validation methods 
including spatial block cross-validation outside the training area.

By the end of the course, you will be able to:

  	* Understand how to assess SDM performance beyond simple metrics like 
AUC and TSS.
  	* Compute a wide variety of metrics in R and apply them to species 
with different ecological and biogeographical traits.
  	* Perform validation of models using spatial blocks and interpret 
reliability and calibration of predictions.

	* Navigate workflows in R tailored to SDM validation and evaluation.

For any questions, please write to courses at transmittingscience.com

Best regards,

Haris

-- 
Haris Saslis, PhD
Course Coordinator
Transmitting Science
www.transmittingscience.com [2]

Links:
------
[1] 
https://www.transmittingscience.com/courses/ecology/evaluating-and-validating-species-distribution-models-with-r/

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] variances and test of their similarity
messages.subject_clean: variances and test of their similarity
messages.body_plain:
Dear all,

could you please help me to interpret variances and their (dis-)similarity?

I have two sets of values, each taken from the same set of subjects. The 
first set of values is taken before some treatment, the other set is 
taken afterwards, so I have two values per subject.

There are 102 subjects.
Variance before is: 0.2028815
Variance after is: 0.1643807

Looking at the qq-norm, I see no strong deviation from normal dist., in 
any of the data groups (before and after).

I have also tried DHARMa, simulating residuals from a model:
plot(simulateResiduals(lm(before ~ 1)))
plot(simulateResiduals(lm(after ~ 1)))
still no warnings.

In that case, I would expect var.test and bartlett.test to be OK.
These tests both reported no significant difference in variances:

 > var.test(before, after)
F = 1.2342, num df = 101, denom df = 101, p-value = 0.292
alternative hypothesis: true ratio of variances is not equal to 1
95 percent confidence interval:
  0.8337509 1.8270352
sample estimates:
ratio of variances
           1.234217

 > bartlett.test(list(before, after))
Bartlett's K-squared = 1.1106, df = 1, p-value = 0.2919

In that case, I would expect Levene's and Fligner-Killeen's test to say 
the same thing, but they did not:

 > fligner.test(list(before, after))
Fligner-Killeen:med chi-squared = 4.4478, df = 1, p-value = 0.03495

Levene's Test for Homogeneity of Variance (center = median)
        Df F value  Pr(>F)
group   1  3.3713 0.06781 .
       202

What does it mean? I have thought that Levene's and Fligner-Killeen's 
are a bit weaker, but useful for non-normal data, so I would expect them 
to keep high probability of the hypothesis that the variances were the 
same (not suggesting rejection), if their stronger counterparts in this 
case (Bartlett and F) did not not suggest to reject it as well...

Can someone explain me (or point me to the explanation) what is going on 
here, please?

Thank you!

With regards,

Martin Weiser


-- 
Please expect long response time (3d+) - this is because of family reasons

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] variances and test of their similarity
messages.subject_clean: variances and test of their similarity
messages.body_plain:
Dear Martin,

var,test (the F-test), and Bartlett's test (if there is only two 
groups)? are testing the same hypothesis, thus it is not a surprise that 
they gave the same p-value. This p-value is valid if values ate normally 
distributed in both groups.

So, your question is valid, why tests not assuming normality indicates 
significant departures from equal variances. My tip is that outlier 
values are responsible for this strange result. However, I'm not sure 
that is is sthe best explanation. Anyway, please keep in your mind that 
figures created by DHARMa package are designed to check applicability of 
linear models that nay be more robust against lack of normality than 
homogeneity tests.

Best wishes,

Zoltan

> counterparts in this case (Bartlett and F) did not not suggest to 
> reject it as well...
>
> Can someone explain me (or point me to the explanation) what is going 
> on here, please?
>
> Thank you!
>
> With regards,
>
> Martin Weiser
>
>
-- 
Botta-Duk?t Zolt?n                         Zolt?n Botta-Duk?t
tudom?nyos tan?csad?                       scientific advisor
?kol?giai Kutat?k?zpont                    Centre for Ecological Research
?kol?giai ?s Botanikai Int?zet             Institute of Ecology and Botany
messages.body_snippet:
Dear Martin,

var,test (the F-test), and Bartlett's test (if there is only two 
groups)? are testing the same hypothesis, thus it is not a surprise that 
they gave the same p-value. This p-value is va

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] Last chance: Machine Learning for Computational Biology
messages.subject_clean: Last chance: Machine Learning for Computational Biology
messages.body_plain:
Hello R-SIG-ECOLOGY members

Last chance - Bring practical machine-learning methods to ecological and environmental analysis with R. Machine Learning for Computational Biology 4.0 is a seminar livestreaming July 30 with Nikolay Oskolkov (Lund University). Build neural networks, random forests, clustering, and MCMC workflows in R and Python for complex biological data. Apply these methods to ecological and environmental datasets, including vegetation, soils, hydrology, and genomic data. Strengthen model implementation, troubleshooting, optimisation, and ethical practice through coding exercises and case studies.

Sign up today (https://instats.org/seminar/machine-learning-for-computational-biolo-4) to secure your spot, and please share this opportunity with colleagues and students who might benefit!


Best wishes

Michael Zyphur
Professor and Director
Instats | instats.org
	[[alternative HTML version deleted]]
